exercises.title: Let bindings and conditionals
exercises.prompt: In OCaml, let x = e1 in e2 binds the name x to the value of e1, then evaluates e2. What does the following expression evaluate to? (Give the integer result.)
exercises.codeSnippet:
let x = 5 in
let y = x * x in
if y > 20 then y - x else y + x
hints: Evaluate let bindings top-to-bottom: first x = 5, then y = 5 * 5 = 25.
Now check the condition: is 25 > 20? Yes, so we take the then branch: y - x = 25 - 5.
exercises.explanation: x = 5, y = 25. The condition 25 > 20 is true, so we return y - x = 25 - 5 = 20.

exercises.title: Recursive and tail-recursive factorial
exercises.prompt: Write two OCaml functions: (1) fact : int -> int using direct recursion, and (2) fact_tr : int -> int using tail recursion with an accumulator. Tail-recursive functions don't grow the call stack — OCaml optimises them.
exercises.codeSnippet:
(* Direct recursive factorial *)
let rec fact n = ???

(* Tail-recursive factorial — add an inner helper with accumulator *)
let fact_tr n = ???

(* Test *)
let () =
  Printf.printf "fact 6 = %d\n" (fact 6);
  Printf.printf "fact_tr 6 = %d\n" (fact_tr 6)
hints: For fact: base case is fact 0 = 1. Recursive case: n * fact (n-1).
For fact_tr: define an inner let rec aux n acc = ... where acc accumulates the product.
Call aux n 1 to start the tail-recursive process with accumulator = 1.
exercises.explanation: The tail-recursive version uses an accumulator so the recursive call is the very last operation — no pending multiplication after it returns. OCaml eliminates the stack frame for tail calls, preventing stack overflow on large inputs.

exercises.title: Pattern matching on lists
exercises.prompt: OCaml lists use :: (cons) and [] (nil). Pattern matching with | [] -> ... | h :: t -> ... is the standard way to process them. What does this print?
exercises.codeSnippet:
let rec sum = function
  | [] -> 0
  | h :: t -> h + sum t

let rec length = function
  | [] -> 0
  | _ :: t -> 1 + length t

let lst = [2; 4; 6; 8]
let () = Printf.printf "%d %d\n" (sum lst) (length lst)
hints: sum adds all elements: 2 + 4 + 6 + 8.
length counts elements: there are 4 elements in [2; 4; 6; 8].

exercises.title: Implement map and filter
exercises.prompt: Implement my_map and my_filter from scratch (without List.map or List.filter). These are the two most fundamental higher-order list functions in OCaml. Then use them to: double all numbers in a list, and keep only even numbers.
exercises.codeSnippet:
(* my_map : ('a -> 'b) -> 'a list -> 'b list *)
let rec my_map f lst = ???

(* my_filter : ('a -> bool) -> 'a list -> 'a list *)
let rec my_filter p lst = ???

let () =
  let nums = [1; 2; 3; 4; 5; 6] in
  let doubled = my_map (fun x -> x * 2) nums in
  let evens = my_filter (fun x -> x mod 2 = 0) nums in
  List.iter (fun x -> Printf.printf "%d " x) doubled;
  print_newline ();
  List.iter (fun x -> Printf.printf "%d " x) evens
hints: my_map: base case is my_map f [] = []. Recursive: f h :: my_map f t.
my_filter: base case is []. Recursive: if p h then h :: my_filter p t else my_filter p t.
Note the polymorphic types: 'a -> 'b for map means input and output types can differ.
exercises.explanation: These functions are fundamental to functional programming. map transforms every element; filter selects elements by a predicate. The key insight is that the function f (or predicate p) is a first-class value passed as an argument — this is what makes OCaml a higher-order language.
exercises.solutionCode:
let rec my_map f = function
  | [] -> []
  | h :: t -> f h :: my_map f t

let rec my_filter p = function
  | [] -> []
  | h :: t ->
    if p h then h :: my_filter p t
    else my_filter p t

let () =
  let nums = [1; 2; 3; 4; 5; 6] in
  let doubled = my_map (fun x -> x * 2) nums in
  let evens = my_filter (fun x -> x mod 2 = 0) nums in
  List.iter (fun x -> Printf.printf "%d " x) doubled;  (* 2 4 6 8 10 12 *)
  print_newline ();
  List.iter (fun x -> Printf.printf "%d " x) evens      (* 2 4 6 *)

exercises.title: Merge sort in OCaml
exercises.prompt: Implement merge sort in OCaml. You'll need: (1) split to divide a list into two halves, (2) merge to merge two sorted lists, (3) msort as the top-level divide-and-conquer. This directly maps to the FoCS lecture structure.
exercises.codeSnippet:
(* Split a list into two roughly equal halves *)
let rec split = function
  | [] -> ([], [])
  | [x] -> ([x], [])
  | x :: y :: rest ->
    let (a, b) = split rest in
    (x :: a, y :: b)

(* Merge two sorted lists into one sorted list *)
let rec merge xs ys = ???

(* Merge sort *)
let rec msort lst = ???

let () =
  let sorted = msort [5; 1; 3; 2; 4] in
  List.iter (fun x -> Printf.printf "%d " x) sorted
hints: merge: base cases are when either list is empty. Otherwise compare heads and cons the smaller one.
msort: base cases are [] and [x]. Otherwise split, recursively sort both halves, then merge.
merge ([], ys) = ys and merge (xs, []) = xs.
exercises.explanation: Merge sort achieves O(n log n) by dividing the problem in half each time. The key insight is that merging two sorted lists takes O(n) — the hard work happens on the way back up the recursion tree, not on the way down.
exercises.solutionCode:
let rec split = function
  | [] -> ([], [])
  | [x] -> ([x], [])
  | x :: y :: rest ->
    let (a, b) = split rest in
    (x :: a, y :: b)

let rec merge xs ys =
  match (xs, ys) with
  | ([], _) -> ys
  | (_, []) -> xs
  | (x :: xs', y :: ys') ->
    if x <= y then x :: merge xs' ys
    else y :: merge xs ys'

let rec msort = function
  | [] -> []
  | [x] -> [x]
  | lst ->
    let (a, b) = split lst in
    merge (msort a) (msort b)

let () =
  let sorted = msort [5; 1; 3; 2; 4] in
  List.iter (fun x -> Printf.printf "%d " x) sorted  (* 1 2 3 4 5 *)

exercises.title: Binary search tree with variant types
exercises.prompt: Define a polymorphic binary tree type and implement insert and member for a binary search tree (BST). This covers the key pattern of algebraic data types (variants) in OCaml, which are central to the FoCS course.
exercises.codeSnippet:
(* Define the tree type: either a Leaf or a Node holding a value and two subtrees *)
type 'a tree = ???

(* insert x t: insert x into BST t (no duplicates) *)
let rec insert x t = ???

(* member x t: is x in BST t? *)
let rec member x t = ???

let () =
  let t = List.fold_right insert [5; 3; 7; 1; 4; 6; 8] Leaf in
  Printf.printf "member 4: %b\n" (member 4 t);
  Printf.printf "member 9: %b\n" (member 9 t)
hints: type 'a tree = Leaf | Node of 'a tree * 'a * 'a tree
insert: if x < v go left (insert into left subtree), if x > v go right, if x = v return unchanged.
member: if x = v return true, if x < v search left, if x > v search right, Leaf means false.
exercises.explanation: Variant types (also called sum types or algebraic data types) are one of OCaml's most powerful features. A tree is either empty (Leaf) or a node with a value and two children — this recursive definition maps perfectly to recursive functions. BST invariant: all values in left subtree < root < all values in right subtree.
exercises.solutionCode:
type 'a tree =
  | Leaf
  | Node of 'a tree * 'a * 'a tree

let rec insert x = function
  | Leaf -> Node (Leaf, x, Leaf)
  | Node (l, v, r) ->
    if x < v then Node (insert x l, v, r)
    else if x > v then Node (l, v, insert x r)
    else Node (l, v, r)  (* duplicate: no change *)

let rec member x = function
  | Leaf -> false
  | Node (l, v, r) ->
    if x = v then true
    else if x < v then member x l
    else member x r

let () =
  let t = List.fold_right insert [5; 3; 7; 1; 4; 6; 8] Leaf in
  Printf.printf "member 4: %b\n" (member 4 t);  (* true *)
  Printf.printf "member 9: %b\n" (member 9 t)   (* false *)

exercises.title: Association list lookup
exercises.prompt: An association list (a-list) is a list of (key, value) pairs — the simplest dictionary structure in OCaml. What does this code print?
exercises.codeSnippet:
let dict = [("alice", 90); ("bob", 75); ("charlie", 85); ("bob", 60)]

let result1 = List.assoc "bob" dict
let result2 = List.assoc_opt "dave" dict

let () =
  Printf.printf "%d\n" result1;
  match result2 with
  | Some v -> Printf.printf "found: %d\n" v
  | None   -> print_endline "not found"
hints: List.assoc key lst returns the value for the first matching key. Note 'bob' appears twice — which is found?
List.assoc_opt returns Some v or None (never raises Not_found).
Dave is not in the list, so assoc_opt returns None.
exercises.explanation: List.assoc finds the first matching key — so "bob" maps to 75 (not 60, even though 60 appears later). List.assoc_opt is the safe version returning an option type, which is idiomatic OCaml for operations that may fail. Association lists are O(n) but simple; for O(log n) use the built-in Map functor.

exercises.title: Currying, partial application, and function composition
exercises.prompt: In OCaml every function takes exactly one argument — multi-argument functions are curried by default. Write: (1) add : int -> int -> int, show partial application, (2) compose : ('b -> 'c) -> ('a -> 'b) -> 'a -> 'c. These are core FoCS concepts.
exercises.codeSnippet:
(* add is automatically curried: add : int -> int -> int *)
let add x y = ???

(* Partial application: add5 takes one int and adds 5 *)
let add5 = ???

(* compose f g x = f (g x) *)
let compose f g x = ???

let () =
  Printf.printf "add5 10 = %d\n" (add5 10);
  let double = fun x -> x * 2 in
  let inc = fun x -> x + 1 in
  let double_then_inc = compose inc double in
  Printf.printf "double_then_inc 7 = %d\n" (double_then_inc 7);
  (* Use List.map with partial application *)
  List.iter (fun x -> Printf.printf "%d " x) (List.map (add 10) [1;2;3])
hints: let add x y = x + y — this is automatically curried because OCaml functions take one arg at a time.
let add5 = add 5 — partial application: supply the first argument, get back a function waiting for the second.
compose f g x = f (g x) — apply g first, then f to the result.
exercises.explanation: Currying means every function f : A -> B -> C is really a function that takes A and returns a function B -> C. This enables partial application: add 5 specialises add to a function that adds 5 to whatever it receives. The type of compose is ('b -> 'c) -> ('a -> 'b) -> 'a -> 'c — it is itself a higher-order curried function.
exercises.solutionCode:
let add x y = x + y

let add5 = add 5

let compose f g x = f (g x)

let () =
  Printf.printf "add5 10 = %d\n" (add5 10);           (* 15 *)
  let double = fun x -> x * 2 in
  let inc = fun x -> x + 1 in
  let double_then_inc = compose inc double in
  Printf.printf "double_then_inc 7 = %d\n" (double_then_inc 7);  (* 15 *)
  List.iter (fun x -> Printf.printf "%d " x) (List.map (add 10) [1;2;3])  (* 11 12 13 *)

exercises.title: Lazy sequences: head extraction
exercises.prompt: A lazy sequence (or stream) defers evaluation using a closure. The type below represents an infinite sequence. What does this program print?
hints: from 3 creates Cons(3, fun () -> from 4). head extracts 3.
tail s calls the thunk: tf () = from 4 = Cons(4, fun () -> from 5). head extracts 4.
tail s2 calls from 5, head extracts 5.
exercises.explanation: The key idea: the tail of a sequence is wrapped in fun () -> ... (a thunk). Calling tf () forces evaluation of the next element on demand. This allows infinite structures — the sequence from 3 never terminates, but we only ever evaluate as many elements as we ask for.

exercises.title: Mutable state with refs and while loops
exercises.prompt: OCaml supports imperative programming via ref cells. A ref r holds a mutable value; !r dereferences it; r := v updates it. Write an imperative (loop-based) factorial using ref, then compare it with the recursive version. This illustrates why functional style is often cleaner.
hints: while !i <= n do ... done is the OCaml while loop syntax.
Inside the loop: result := !result * !i; i := !i + 1
!r reads the value of ref r. r := v sets it.
exercises.explanation: Refs introduce mutable state. !r is read access; r := v is write access. The while loop body uses := to update both the accumulator and counter. Compare this to fact_tr — both avoid stack overflow, but the imperative style requires careful management of mutable state, while the tail-recursive style is pure and easier to reason about.
